name: Belgium UFO Wave
description: The Belgium UFO Wave refers to a series of alleged unidentified flying object sightings that occurred primarily between November 1989 and April 1990 in Belgium. It is considered one of the largest documented cases of UFO sightings. Proponents claim that thousands of people witnessed triangular-shaped objects, often described as silent, flying at low altitudes. The sightings were accompanied by radar tracking and photographic evidence, which proponents argue supports the existence of extraterrestrial or advanced aerial vehicles. The Belgian Air Force even scrambled F-16 fighter jets to investigate some of the radar anomalies. However, mainstream explanations suggest the sightings were misidentified conventional aircraft, atmospheric phenomena, or mass hysteria. Skeptics point to the lack of conclusive photographic or video evidence and the potential for psychological factors to influence witness accounts. The official report by General De Brouwer acknowledged the radar data but offered no definitive explanation, fueling both belief and skepticism. The wave remains a significant case study in ufology, highlighting the challenges of interpreting ambiguous aerial phenomena and the complex interplay between perception, technology, and belief.
subtitle: Mass UFO sightings over Belgium

claims.claim: Triangular UFOs were observed by thousands.
claims.criticism: Eyewitness testimony is subjective and prone to error; mass hysteria is a possible explanation.

claims.claim: Radar detected unidentified objects moving in unusual patterns.
claims.evidence: Multiple radar stations tracked objects at high speeds and performing impossible maneuvers.
claims.criticism: Radar anomalies can be caused by atmospheric conditions, equipment malfunctions, or misidentification of known aircraft.

claims.claim: Photographic evidence supports the existence of UFOs.
claims.evidence: Several photographs were taken of alleged triangular objects.
claims.criticism: The photographic evidence is often of poor quality and can be easily explained as hoaxes or misidentified objects, such as aircraft lights.

claims.claim: The Belgian Air Force attempted to intercept the UFOs.
claims.evidence: F-16 fighter jets were scrambled to investigate radar contacts.
claims.criticism: The fighter jets were unable to visually confirm the objects, and the radar contacts could have been due to atmospheric conditions or electronic countermeasures.

keyFigures.name: General Wilfried De Brouwer
keyFigures.role: Chief of Operations of the Belgian Air Force, oversaw the investigation

keyFigures.name: August Meessen
keyFigures.role: Physicist who investigated and criticized the official explanation

keyFigures.name: Renaud Leclet
keyFigures.role: Ufologist who documented and promoted the case

relatedConcepts: Close Encounters of the Third Kind

timeline.event: Initial reports of triangular UFO sightings begin.
Peak of the wave with widespread sightings and radar confirmations.
Belgian Air Force releases official report acknowledging radar data but offering no definitive explanation.
The Belgium UFO Wave remains a subject of debate and investigation within ufology.

sources.author: Renaud Leclet
sources.work: Numerous articles and presentations on the Belgium UFO Wave
sources.title: Renaud Leclet

sources.author: Steuart Campbell
sources.work: UFOs and the Limits of Science
sources.title: Steuart Campbell
